title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Ben Fowler, who makes ceramic bowls and plates.
audio_script: Interviewer: Our guest today is Ben Fowler, who makes ceramic bowls and plates in his studio. Ben, how did you start working with clay? || Guest: I tried it on an evening course while I was working in a bank. [20]I enjoyed the way you could make something useful with your hands after spending all day on a computer[/20]. That feeling kept bringing me back. || Interviewer: Was it difficult to leave your old job? || Guest: Yes, because it was safe and I had good colleagues. [21]The hardest thing was not knowing whether people would buy enough of my work to pay the bills[/21]. I had to save money first. || Interviewer: What do you like about making everyday objects? || Guest: Some artists prefer pieces that sit on a shelf, and I understand that. But [22]I like knowing that someone will use my bowl for breakfast or soup, not just look at it[/22]. || Interviewer: What often goes wrong in the studio? || Guest: Lots of things. A plate can crack while drying, or colours can change in the kiln. [23]You have to accept that the heat sometimes changes your plan completely[/23]. That's frustrating but also interesting. || Interviewer: How do you get ideas for new designs? || Guest: I don't draw very detailed plans. [24]Most ideas come from shapes I notice outside, especially stones, shells and leaves[/24]. I take photos and use them later. || Interviewer: What are you trying to improve at the moment? || Guest: I'm working on making larger pieces. [25]Small bowls are comfortable for me now, but I want to make big serving dishes without them becoming too heavy[/25]. That's a real challenge. || Interviewer: Do you sell mainly online? || Guest: I sell some pieces online, but markets are better for me. People like picking things up and feeling the weight. They also ask about how pieces are made, and those conversations often lead to sales. Online photos don't show everything. || Interviewer: What advice would you give someone starting pottery? || Guest: Don't expect perfect results quickly. Clay teaches patience. Also, keep your first pieces even if they look terrible. Later, when you improve, they remind you how far you've come. I still have an uneven little cup from my first class, and I use it for pencils. || Interviewer: How long does one bowl take to make? || Guest: People are surprised that it is not just one afternoon. A bowl has to be shaped, dried slowly, fired, glazed and fired again. If you rush, it may crack. So even a simple bowl can take weeks from start to finish. || Interviewer: Do you mind when pieces break? || Guest: I mind less than I used to. If something breaks, I try to understand why. Sometimes the mistake leads to a better idea, although I still feel disappointed if it was a piece I really liked. || Interviewer: Do you teach pottery as well? || Guest: I run a beginner class once a month. Teaching reminds me how difficult the first steps are. Adults often want perfect bowls immediately, so I try to make them laugh and enjoy the process rather than worry about every mistake. || Interviewer: Where do you hope your work will be in five years? || Guest: I'd like it to be in more homes, not just galleries.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Our guest today is Ben Fowler, who makes ceramic bowls and plates in his studio. Ben, how did you start working with clay?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I tried it on an evening course while I was working in a bank. I enjoyed the way you could make something useful with your hands after spending all day on a computer. That feeling kept bringing me back.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Was it difficult to leave your old job?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, because it was safe and I had good colleagues. The hardest thing was not knowing whether people would buy enough of my work to pay the bills. I had to save money first.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you like about making everyday objects?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Some artists prefer pieces that sit on a shelf, and I understand that. But I like knowing that someone will use my bowl for breakfast or soup, not just look at it.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What often goes wrong in the studio?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Lots of things. A plate can crack while drying, or colours can change in the kiln. You have to accept that the heat sometimes changes your plan completely. That's frustrating but also interesting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you get ideas for new designs?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I don't draw very detailed plans. Most ideas come from shapes I notice outside, especially stones, shells and leaves. I take photos and use them later.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What are you trying to improve at the moment?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I'm working on making larger pieces. Small bowls are comfortable for me now, but I want to make big serving dishes without them becoming too heavy. That's a real challenge.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you sell mainly online?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I sell some pieces online, but markets are better for me. People like picking things up and feeling the weight. They also ask about how pieces are made, and those conversations often lead to sales. Online photos don't show everything.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What advice would you give someone starting pottery?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Don't expect perfect results quickly. Clay teaches patience. Also, keep your first pieces even if they look terrible. Later, when you improve, they remind you how far you've come. I still have an uneven little cup from my first class, and I use it for pencils.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How long does one bowl take to make?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People are surprised that it is not just one afternoon. A bowl has to be shaped, dried slowly, fired, glazed and fired again. If you rush, it may crack. So even a simple bowl can take weeks from start to finish.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you mind when pieces break?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I mind less than I used to. If something breaks, I try to understand why. Sometimes the mistake leads to a better idea, although I still feel disappointed if it was a piece I really liked.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you teach pottery as well?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I run a beginner class once a month. Teaching reminds me how difficult the first steps are. Adults often want perfect bowls immediately, so I try to make them laugh and enjoy the process rather than worry about every mistake.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Where do you hope your work will be in five years?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I'd like it to be in more homes, not just galleries.

questions.question: Why did Ben enjoy his first pottery course?

options.A: It helped him relax with colleagues.
options.B: It let him make useful things by hand.
options.C: It gave him a break from studying.

questions.explanation: Ben says he enjoyed making something useful with his hands after a day on a computer.

questions.question: What worried Ben about leaving his bank job?

options.C: Earning enough money from his work.

questions.explanation: He says the hardest thing was not knowing whether people would buy enough work to pay the bills.

questions.question: What does Ben like about making bowls and plates?

options.A: People can use them in daily life.
options.B: They are easier to sell online.
options.C: They take less time than other pieces.

questions.explanation: He likes knowing someone will use his bowl for breakfast or soup.

questions.question: What does Ben say about using the kiln?

options.A: It makes colours easier to control.
options.B: It can change what he intended to make.
options.C: It is the quickest part of the process.

questions.explanation: Ben says the heat sometimes changes his plan completely.

questions.question: Where does Ben get many of his design ideas?

options.C: From natural shapes he sees outside.

questions.explanation: He says most ideas come from shapes outside, especially stones, shells and leaves.

questions.question: What is Ben trying to do now?

options.B: Produce more small bowls each week.

questions.explanation: He says he wants to make big serving dishes without them becoming too heavy.
